explanations.A: Correct. The expected next value equals the current value.
explanations.B: 0 holds only if the current value is 0.
explanations.C: X₀ is the unconditional mean, not the one-step conditional expectation.
explanations.D: n is the time index, unrelated to the conditional mean.

questions.question: For a martingale, the expected one-step increment E[Xₙ₊₁ − Xₙ | history] is:

explanations.A: Correct. Zero drift is the defining feature.
explanations.B: Xₙ is the expected next value, not the change.
explanations.C: A drift of +1 makes it a submartingale.
explanations.D: A drift of −1 makes it a supermartingale.

questions.question: Which is a martingale?
options: Wealth in a game with a house edge

explanations.A: Correct. Equal up/down probabilities give zero drift.
explanations.C: The running maximum never decreases, so it has upward drift.
explanations.D: A house edge gives negative drift (supermartingale).

questions.question: A bet wins +$6 or loses −$6 with equal probability. Expected profit per bet:

explanations.D: $3 halves the stake without accounting for the loss.

questions.question: Start at $40 and make 50 fair ±$1 bets. Expected ending bankroll:

explanations.A: Correct. Zero-drift steps leave the expectation at the start, $40.
explanations.B: $0 confuses expected increment with expected bankroll.

questions.question: By the Optional Stopping Theorem, for a martingale X and a suitable τ, E[X_τ] equals:

explanations.A: Correct. Stopping a fair game at a valid rule preserves the starting expectation.
explanations.C: E[τ] is the expected stopping time.
explanations.D: A barrier is an outcome, not the expected stopping value.

questions.question: Which is a valid stopping time?
options: First time the walk hits 0 or 100
One step before the all-time high
The moment of the eventual maximum

explanations.A: Correct. A hitting time uses only past/present information.
explanations.B: Knowing the all-time high requires the future.
explanations.C: The eventual maximum is only known in hindsight.
explanations.D: Anticipating the next loss peeks ahead.

questions.question: Fair walk from 2, barriers at 0 and 10. P(reach 10 first)?
Fair walk from 7, barriers at 0 and 10. P(ruin = reach 0 first)?

explanations.B: 0.7 is the probability of reaching 10, not ruin.

questions.question: Start $25, fair $1 bets, quit at $0 or $100. P(reach $100)?
Fair walk starts at 0, stops at +1 or −4. P(hit +1 first)?

explanations.A: Correct. Distance to lower barrier 4 over width 5 gives 4/5.

questions.question: Symmetric walk from 3, barriers 0 and 10. Expected steps to absorption?

explanations.D: 9 = 3² is the squared start, not the duration.

questions.question: Symmetric walk from 0, barriers +3 and −5. Expected duration?
Symmetric walk starting at the midpoint of barriers 0 and 6. Expected duration?

explanations.B: 6 is the width, not the product.

questions.question: Expected profit from any valid stopping strategy in a strictly fair game:
options: Positive if you quit while ahead

explanations.A: Correct. OST gives E[X_τ] = X₀, so expected profit is 0.
explanations.B: A fair game cannot be made positive in expectation.
explanations.D: Quitting while ahead is offset by rare deep losses; expectation stays 0.

questions.question: The doubling betting system fails to beat a fair game because it requires:
options: Unbounded bankroll and time (OST hypotheses fail)
Breaking the law of large numbers
A casino with no table limit only

explanations.A: Correct. The 'sure win' needs infinite money and time, which is exactly what OST rules out.
explanations.B: It fails even with a perfectly fair coin.
explanations.C: The law of large numbers is not the issue.
explanations.D: Table limits are a practical detail, not the core reason.

questions.question: Wealth under bets with a small house edge is best described as a:

explanations.A: Correct. A house edge gives negative drift, a supermartingale.
explanations.B: A martingale needs a perfectly fair game.
explanations.C: A submartingale drifts up, favoring the player.
explanations.D: Wealth fluctuates, so it is not constant.

questions.question: A martingale starts at X₀ = 12. For any valid stopping time τ, E[X_τ] = ?
